user_prompt: Explain Bernoulli’s principle using an airplane wing in outside flight. Visualize airflow speed and pressure differences over and under the wing. No humans on screen. Explanation must be delivered only through off-screen voiceover narration.

style_bible.visual_style: Realistic 3D rendering with clear, vivid colors emphasizing airflow visualization using transparent and colored particle effects around the airplane wing.
style_bible.camera_rules: Camera remains outside the aircraft, never showing any human or cockpit interior, using smooth continuous movements to reveal airflow and pressure differences.
style_bible.lighting_rules: Soft natural daylight with slight directional sunlight from above-left to enhance wing contours and airflow visualization, balanced shadows for depth.
style_bible.continuity_rules: Primary subject and environment remain consistent across scenes; airflow visualization style and wing model remain identical; camera moves smoothly without abrupt cuts.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Show an airplane wing in steady flight with airflow approaching and passing around it, establishing baseline airflow conditions.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY:
- primary subject: airplane wing
- environment: clear blue sky with distant clouds
- explanatory state: baseline condition (observation)

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: medium shot, wing centered horizontally with leading edge on left
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow dolly forward
- subject & action: airflow particles begin to approach wing smoothly by 1s, by 3s particles flow evenly over and under the wing
- environment details: soft clouds in background, clear blue sky, distant horizon line
- lighting: soft directional sunlight from above-left, natural daylight color temperature
- cinematic look: clean, high contrast with shallow depth of field on wing edges

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: medium-close, focus on wing cross-section from side
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow pan left to right following airflow
- subject & action: airflow particles continue steady flow, showing smooth streamlines over and under wing
- environment details: same sky and clouds, subtle wing shadow on underside
- lighting: consistent soft sunlight, slight rim light on wing upper surface
- cinematic look: sharp details on wing surface, clear particle trails

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: close-up on wing upper and lower surfaces
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: static
- subject & action: airflow particles visible with uniform speed, no pressure indicators yet
- environment details: blue sky background blurred
- lighting: soft sunlight highlighting wing curvature
- cinematic look: crisp focus on wing textures and airflow

AVOID: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.negative_prompt: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.camera: Slow dolly forward to medium shot, then slow pan left to right, ending with static close-up
scenes.audio_cue: Here we see an airplane wing in steady flight, with air flowing smoothly around it. Notice the airflow moving evenly over and under the wing, setting the stage to understand how this movement affects flight.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Reveal the cause of lift by visualizing airflow speed differences and pressure zones above and below the wing.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY:
- primary subject: airplane wing
- environment: clear blue sky with distant clouds
- explanatory state: mechanism revealed (cause)

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: medium shot, wing centered horizontally
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow dolly backward
- subject & action: airflow particles accelerate over the curved upper wing surface by 2s, slower under the flatter lower surface; colored pressure zones appear (blue for low pressure above, red for higher pressure below)
- environment details: same sky and clouds
- lighting: soft directional sunlight from above-left
- cinematic look: enhanced particle effects with color-coded pressure visualization

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: medium-close side view of wing cross-section
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow pan right to left following airflow
- subject & action: airflow speed vectors appear, longer arrows over upper surface indicating faster speed; pressure gradient arrows point from high to low pressure
- environment details: sky background with subtle cloud shapes
- lighting: consistent soft sunlight
- cinematic look: clear visualization of forces and speeds with semi-transparent overlays

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: close-up on wing upper surface
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: static
- subject & action: airflow particles visibly curve and speed up over wing, pressure difference shading remains stable
- environment details: blurred sky background
- lighting: soft sunlight highlighting wing curvature
- cinematic look: sharp focus on airflow and pressure zones

AVOID: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.negative_prompt: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.camera: Slow dolly backward, then slow pan right to left, ending with static close-up
scenes.audio_cue: The wing's curved upper surface causes air to speed up, creating lower pressure above, while slower air below maintains higher pressure. This pressure difference is what generates lift, pushing the wing upward.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Show the effect of pressure difference producing lift force on the airplane wing.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY:
- primary subject: airplane wing
- environment: clear blue sky with distant clouds
- explanatory state: outcome explained (effect)

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: medium shot, wing angled slightly upward
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow crane up
- subject & action: pressure zones remain visible; animated force vector arrow appears perpendicular and upward from wing surface indicating lift
- environment details: sky and clouds consistent
- lighting: soft directional sunlight
- cinematic look: clear visualization of force with glowing arrow

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: medium-close side view
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow pan left to right
- subject & action: airflow and pressure zones stable; force arrow pulses gently to emphasize lift magnitude
- environment details: subtle cloud shapes
- lighting: consistent sunlight
- cinematic look: semi-transparent overlays for airflow and force

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: close-up on wing tip
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: static
- subject & action: final frame holds on wing tip with airflow and lift vector clearly visible
- environment details: blurred blue sky
- lighting: soft sunlight highlighting wing edges
- cinematic look: sharp focus with slight glow on force arrow

AVOID: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.negative_prompt: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.camera: Slow crane up, then slow pan left to right, ending with static close-up
scenes.audio_cue: This pressure difference creates an upward lift force on the wing, allowing the airplane to rise and stay aloft. Understanding this principle is key to how flight works.
